title: Effectively Existentially-Atomic Structures
overall_summary: The paper isolates and studies existentially atomic structures: countable structures in which every finite tuple has its automorphism orbit defined by a first-order existential formula. It proves that this model-theoretic condition is equivalent to several descriptive and computability-theoretic simplicity properties, including having an infinitary Π2 Scott sentence, uniform continuous categoricity, support of realized universal types by existential formulas, and a one-quantifier version of primeness. The effective version identifies computable structures with c.e. existential Scott families with uniformly relatively computably categorical structures and uniformly effectively 1-prime structures; with finite parameters, existential atomicity is equivalent to having an infinitary Σ3 Scott sentence and to computable categoricity on a cone. The main proof tools are back-and-forth relations generated by existential orbit formulas, a type-omitting theorem for unsupported universal types in Π2 classes, and a finite-use/permutation argument extracting existential definitions from uniform computable operators. For the original problem, the paper is most useful as a bridge between computability-on-a-cone phenomena and Σ1-in-style existential definability of orbits or graphs, and its Lemma 6.2 gives a particularly relevant template for turning uniform computability of maps into existential definitions by analyzing finite oracle use.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Scott-family observation from Definitions 1.1, 1.5, and 1.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a countable structure A, let orb_A(a) be the set of tuples b of the same length as a such that some automorphism of A maps a to b. A is existentially atomic, meaning that for every finite tuple a from A there is a first-order existential formula φ_a(x) whose realizations in A are exactly orb_A(a), if and only if A has a Scott family consisting of existential formulas. Here a Scott family is a set S of formulas such that every finite tuple from A satisfies some formula in S, and any two tuples satisfying the same formula in S are automorphic in A. A computable structure is effectively existentially atomic if it has a c.e. Scott family of existential formulas, where c.e. refers to the set of indices for the formulas.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If each orbit orb_A(a) is defined by an existential formula φ_a, then the collection of all such formulas is a Scott family: every tuple a satisfies φ_a, and if two tuples satisfy φ_a then both are in the orbit of a, hence are automorphic. Conversely, suppose S is a Scott family of existential formulas. Given a tuple a, choose θ in S with A models θ(a). If b satisfies θ, the Scott-family property gives an automorphism sending a to b. If b is automorphic to a, then b satisfies θ because formulas without parameters are invariant under automorphisms. Thus θ defines exactly orb_A(a). The effective version is just the same equivalence with the additional requirement that the existential Scott family be computably enumerable.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure. The following are equivalent. (A1) A is existentially atomic: every finite tuple a has its automorphism orbit orb_A(a) defined in A by a first-order existential formula. (A2) A has an infinitary Π2 Scott sentence, i.e. an L_{ω1,ω} Scott sentence of Π2 complexity whose countable models are exactly the copies of A. (A3) A is uniformly continuously categorical: there is a continuous operator Φ from atomic diagrams of copies B of A to functions ω to ω such that Φ^{D(B)} is an isomorphism from B to A. (A4) Every first-order type realized in A is existentially supported in A: for every realized type p(x), there is an existential formula φ(x), realized in A, such that A models ∀x(φ(x) implies ψ(x)) for every ψ in p. (A5) Every universal type realized in A is existentially supported in A, in the same sense but only for types consisting of universal formulas. (A6) A is 1-prime: for every countable model B of the ∀2-theory of A, there is an embedding f from A into B that preserves universal formulas; the ∀2-theory consists of all sentences of the form ∀y ψ(y), with ψ existential, true in A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implications A1 implies A4 and A4 implies A5 are immediate: if an existential formula defines the automorphism orbit of a tuple a, then every formula true of a is implied by that orbit formula inside A; universal types are special cases of first-order types. The nontrivial implication A5 implies A1 is proved in Section 4 by choosing, for each tuple a, an existential formula φ_a supporting its universal type and then showing that I_A = {(a,b): A models φ_a(b)} has the back-and-forth property; this forces tuples satisfying φ_a to be automorphic to a. The implication A1 implies A2 is Lemma 7.1: use the existential orbit formulas to write a Π2 Scott sentence asserting that the relation I_B = {(a,b): B models φ_a(b)} has the back-and-forth property. The converse A2 implies A1 uses Lemma 5.3: if A were not existentially atomic, then by A1 implies A5 there would be a realized universal type not existentially supported; omitting that type inside the Π2 Scott class would produce a countable model of the Scott sentence not isomorphic to A. The equivalence A1 iff A6 is Lemma 6.1: existential orbit formulas let one build a universal-formula-preserving embedding into any model of the ∀2-theory, while failure of existential atomicity yields, via type omission, a model of the ∀2-theory omitting a universal type realized in A, so no such embedding can exist. The equivalence with A3 is stated as part of the theorem; the paper notes that Melnikov and Montalbán proved the equivalence between A2 and A3 in a more general Polish-group-action setting as an easy consequence of Effros's theorem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If A and B are countable existentially algebraic structures with the same existential theories, then A and B are isomorphic. An element a of a structure A is existentially algebraic if there is an existential formula φ(x) true of a such that the set {b in A: A models φ(b)} is finite; A is existentially algebraic if every element of A is existentially algebraic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Enumerate A as a_0, a_1, ... . For each n choose an existential formula φ_n(x_0,...,x_{n-1}) true of the tuple a_0,...,a_{n-1}, with only finitely many solutions in A, and implying φ_{n-1} on the initial coordinates. Such formulas are obtained by combining finite-solution existential formulas for the individual entries. Define a tree T of finite tuples b from B by putting b of length n into T iff the finite atomic diagram of b in B agrees with that of a_0,...,a_{n-1} in A and B models φ_n(b). The tree is finitely branching because each φ_n has finitely many solutions. It is infinite because, for each n, A satisfies the existential sentence saying that there is a tuple with the relevant atomic diagram and satisfying φ_n; since A and B have the same existential theory, B satisfies the same sentence. By König's lemma T has an infinite path P. The map a_n ↦ P(n) preserves all finite atomic diagrams, so it is an embedding of A into B. To see it is onto, take b in B and an existential formula θ with finitely many solutions in B and true of b. The number of solutions to θ is determined by the existential theory via sentences asserting at least k distinct solutions; hence A and B have the same finite number of θ-solutions. Embeddings preserve existential formulas, so the image already accounts for all θ-solutions in B, and b must be in the image.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every existentially algebraic countable structure is existentially atomic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a finite tuple a in an existentially algebraic structure A. Choose an existential formula φ(x) true of a with the least possible finite number k of solutions in A. The claim is that φ defines the automorphism orbit of a. Suppose b is another solution of φ but b is not automorphic to a. Then the expanded structures (A,a) and (A,b) are both existentially algebraic and not isomorphic, so by Lemma 3.3 they cannot have the same existential theory. Hence some existential formula ψ holds of one of a,b but not the other. If ψ holds of a and not of b, then φ ∧ ψ is an existential formula true of a with fewer than k solutions, contradicting minimality. If ψ holds of b and not of a, let i be the finite number of solutions of φ ∧ ψ. The formula saying φ(y) and there exist i distinct solutions to φ ∧ ψ all different from y is existential, is true of a, and has exactly k − i solutions among the φ-solutions, again contradicting minimality. Therefore every solution of φ is automorphic to a, so φ is an existential orbit definition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Back-and-forth observation after Definition 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A and B be countable structures. A set I ⊆ A^{<ω} × B^{<ω} has the back-and-forth property if for every pair (a,b) in I, the tuples a and b have the same finite atomic diagram, for every c in A there is d in B with (a c, b d) in I, and for every d in B there is c in A with (a c, b d) in I. If I has the back-and-forth property and (a,b) is in I, then there is an isomorphism from A to B mapping a to b. If I is c.e. in the relevant presentations, the isomorphism can be constructed computably.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Starting with the finite partial map sending a to b, alternately satisfy domain and range requirements. At a domain step, for the next element c of A not yet in the domain, use the forth condition to extend the current tuple pair to (a c, b d). At a range step, for the next element d of B not yet in the range, use the back condition to find a matching c. The atomic-diagram condition ensures that every finite partial map constructed is a partial isomorphism. The union of the increasing finite partial isomorphisms is a total bijective isomorphism. If I is c.e., one searches effectively for the required witnesses at each step.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4 proposition: A5 implies A1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If every universal type realized in a countable structure A is existentially supported in A, then A is existentially atomic. More explicitly, if for every finite tuple a there is an existential formula φ_a(x) that is realized in A and implies every universal formula true of a inside A, then the formulas φ_a can be chosen so that each φ_a defines the automorphism orbit of a.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each tuple a choose an existential formula φ_a supporting the universal type of a. First, A models φ_a(a): otherwise ¬φ_a, which is universal because φ_a is existential, would belong to the universal type of a and would be implied by φ_a, contradicting realizability of φ_a. Next prove symmetry: if A models φ_a(b), then A models φ_b(a). If not, ¬φ_b is in the universal type of a, so φ_a implies ¬φ_b; but b satisfies φ_a and, by the previous fact, b also satisfies φ_b. Define I_A = {(a,b): A models φ_a(b)}. If (a,b) is in I_A, then a and b satisfy the same universal formulas, using support and symmetry; hence they have the same atomic diagram. For the forth property, fix c in A. If no d satisfies φ_{a c}(b,d), then A models ¬∃y φ_{a c}(b,y), a universal formula in the universal type of b. Since φ_b supports that type and φ_b(a) holds by symmetry, A would model ¬∃y φ_{a c}(a,y), contradicting the witness c and the fact that φ_{a c}(a,c) holds. The back property follows symmetrically. Therefore I_A has the back-and-forth property, and any b satisfying φ_a is automorphic to a. Thus the φ_a form existential orbit definitions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume, without loss of generality, that the vocabulary is relational. Let K be a Πc_2 class of structures, meaning K is axiomatized by a computable infinitary Π2 sentence, and let K_fin = {D_A(a): A in K and a is a finite tuple from A} be the set of finite atomic diagrams realized in members of K. If K_fin is c.e., then K contains a computable structure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write the Πc_2 axiom for K as a computable conjunction over i of sentences ∀y_i ψ_i(y_i), where each ψ_i is a computable infinitary Σ1 formula. Build a nested sequence of finite partial diagrams σ_s in K_fin; let A_s be the corresponding finite partial structure. At stage s+1, extend A_s to A_{s+1} so that for every i < s and every tuple a from A_s of the right arity, A_{s+1} already contains finite witnesses making ψ_i(a) true. Such an extension can be found by searching the c.e. set K_fin. Existence of the desired extension follows because A_s is in K_fin, so it appears as a finite substructure of some B in K; since B satisfies all ∀y_i ψ_i(y_i), finitely many witnesses in B suffice for the finitely many current requirements, and a finite substructure containing them gives an element of K_fin. The limit atomic diagram D(A) = union_s σ_s is computable because the construction is effective and nested, and A satisfies every Π2 requirement because each tuple and each i is eventually handled.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a Πc_2 class of structures, and let S be the existential theory of some structure in K. If S is c.e. in an oracle X, then there is an X-computable presentation of a structure in K whose existential theory is exactly S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Expand the Πc_2 axiomatization of K by a Πc,X_2 condition forcing the existential theory to be S. First include every existential sentence in S as a required sentence. Second, for every finite tuple x, require that its finite atomic diagram be one whose existential realization sentence belongs to S; formally this is expressed using a disjunction over finite atomic diagrams σ such that the existential sentence saying some tuple realizes σ is in S. Call the resulting class K_S. It is nonempty because S came from a member of K, and its finite diagrams are enumeration reducible to S, hence c.e. in X. Applying Lemma 5.1 relative to X yields an X-computable model in K_S, which has precisely the existential theory S.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a Πin_2 class of structures, and let {p_i(x_i): i in ω} be a sequence of universal types, each not existentially supported in K. Then there is a structure A in K omitting every type p_i. Moreover, if K is Πc_2, K_fin is c.e., and the list of the types p_i is c.e., then A can be chosen computable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Construct a structure by finite approximations as in Lemma 5.1. Even stages meet the Π2 axioms of K by adding finite witnesses for the finitely many currently relevant Σ1 requirements. Odd stages diagonalize against the types. At an odd stage assigned to the pair (i,j), take the j-th tuple a in the current finite approximation A_s and write the rest of A_s as b. Let σ be the finite atomic diagram D_{A_s}(a,b), so a satisfies the existential formula ∃y φ^at_σ(a,y) in the current approximation. Because p_i is not existentially supported in K, some universal formula ψ in p_i is not implied within K by this existential formula. Hence there is a member of K with a tuple realizing ∃y φ^at_σ but failing ψ; by taking a suitable finite substructure and renaming, obtain a finite extension B of A_s in K_fin in which a already has a finite witness to ¬ψ. Set A_{s+1}=B. Since ψ is universal, its failure is existentially witnessed and persists to the limit. In the effective case, one searches through the c.e. list of formulas in p_i and the c.e. set K_fin until such a finite extension is found.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A countable structure A is existentially atomic if and only if it is 1-prime. Here A is 1-prime if for every countable model B of the ∀2-theory of A there is an embedding f:A→B preserving universal formulas, called a 1-embedding.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume A is existentially atomic. Enumerate A as a_1,a_2,..., and let φ_s be an existential formula defining the orbit of the initial tuple a_1,...,a_s. Given a model B of the ∀2-theory of A, define f by stages, maintaining B models φ_s(f(a_1),...,f(a_s)). The extension from s to s+1 is justified because A satisfies the appropriate ∀2 sentence saying that any tuple satisfying φ_s can be extended to one satisfying φ_{s+1}; syntactically, implications from existential antecedents are rewritten by universally quantifying over witnesses to the antecedent, giving a ∀2 sentence. The resulting embedding preserves atomic facts because the orbit formulas imply the relevant finite atomic diagrams. It preserves universal formulas because if a universal formula ψ holds of a_1,...,a_s in A, then A satisfies the ∀2 consequence that φ_s implies ψ, and so B satisfies the same consequence. Conversely, if A is not existentially atomic, then by the previously proved implication from existential atomicity to support of universal types, some universal type realized by a tuple a in A is not existentially supported in A. Let K be the class of countable models of the ∀2-theory of A. Lemma 5.3 gives a model B of K omitting the universal type of a. Any 1-embedding A→B would preserve that universal type and thus would have to send a to a realization of it in B, impossible. Hence A is not 1-prime.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a computable structure A, A is effectively existentially atomic if and only if A is uniformly effectively 1-prime. Uniformly effectively 1-prime means that there is a computable operator Φ such that for every computable model B of the ∀2-theory of A, Φ^{D(B)} is a 1-embedding from A into B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the left-to-right direction, use a c.e. existential Scott family for A to carry out the construction from Lemma 6.1 uniformly in the atomic diagram D(B). At each stage search for an existential orbit formula for the next initial segment and for a tuple in B satisfying it; the ∀2-theory guarantees success. This yields a 1-embedding computable uniformly from D(B). For the right-to-left direction, let Φ witness uniform effective 1-primeness. Apply Φ to D(A), obtaining a 1-embedding of A into itself. For an initial segment a = (a_0,...,a_{m-1}) of the fixed computable enumeration of A, the proof chooses a tuple ã with Φ^{D(A)}(ã) = (0,1,...,m−1). Let s be a finite use of the computation on ã, and let c = (a_m,...,a_{s-1}) so that a c is the first s elements. Define an existential formula φ_a(x) as ∃y φ^at_{D(A) restricted to s}(x,y), where φ^at encodes the finite atomic diagram of the first s elements of A. If b in A satisfies φ_a, take witnesses d such that b d has the same finite atomic diagram as a c. Form a new presentation Ã of A by permuting a c to b d and leaving the rest fixed. Since D(Ã) agrees with D(A) on the first s bits used by the computation, Φ^{D(Ã)}(ã) has the same numerical value as Φ^{D(A)}(ã), but in the new presentation that value denotes b rather than a. Because both Φ^{D(A)} and Φ^{D(Ã)} preserve universal types, a and b have the same universal type. Thus φ_a supports the universal type of a. Uniformly over initial segments, this gives a c.e. family of existential formulas supporting universal types, hence by the Section 4 argument a c.e. existential Scott family.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every existentially atomic countable structure has an infinitary Π2 Scott sentence. Moreover, every effectively existentially atomic computable structure has a computable infinitary Πc_2 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let S be an existential Scott family for A, and choose for each finite tuple a an existential formula φ_a defining its orbit. For any structure B, define I_B = {(a,b): b is a tuple from B of the same length as a and B models φ_a(b)}. Since the analogous relation I_A has the back-and-forth property, B is isomorphic to A exactly when I_B has the back-and-forth property. Write a single infinitary sentence asserting this: for every finite tuple a from A and every tuple x in the candidate structure, if φ_a(x) holds, then x has the same finite atomic diagram as a, every element y of the candidate can be matched by some c in A with φ_{a c}(x y), and for every c in A there exists y in the candidate with φ_{a c}(x y). Because the φ_a are existential, this back-and-forth assertion has Π2 infinitary complexity. In the computable effective case, A is computable and the existential Scott family is c.e.; by searching for a formula in the family true of each tuple, one can choose the map a ↦ φ_a computably enough to make the displayed infinitary conjunctions and disjunctions computable, yielding a Πc_2 Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proof of A2 implies A1 in Section 7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If a countable structure A has an infinitary Π2 Scott sentence, then A is existentially atomic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let ψ be a Πin_2 Scott sentence for A. Suppose A is not existentially atomic. Since existential atomicity implies existential support of all realized universal types, some universal type p realized in A is not existentially supported. Apply Lemma 5.3 to the Πin_2 class of models of ψ and the single type p. This yields a countable model B of ψ omitting p. But ψ is a Scott sentence, so every countable model of ψ must be isomorphic to A; an isomorphic copy of A realizes exactly the same universal types as A, including p. This contradiction shows that no such unsupported p exists, and hence A is existentially atomic by the Section 4 proposition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a countable structure A, the following are equivalent: (1) A is existentially atomic over a finite tuple of parameters, meaning that some finite expansion (A,a) by constants for a tuple a is existentially atomic; (2) A has an infinitary Σ3 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If A is existentially atomic over a finite tuple a, then by Lemma 7.1 the expanded structure (A,a) has a Πin_2 Scott sentence θ(c) in the language with constants c for the tuple. Then ∃y θ(y) is a Σin_3 Scott sentence for the reduct A, since it asserts that some tuple names parameters making the expansion a copy of (A,a). Conversely, suppose A has a Σin_3 Scott sentence. Write it as a countable disjunction of sentences ∃y_i ψ_i(y_i), with each ψ_i Πin_2. Since A satisfies one disjunct, that single disjunct is itself a Scott sentence for A. Fix a tuple a in A witnessing A models ψ(a), and add constant symbols c of the same length. The sentence ψ(c) need not be a Scott sentence for the expansion (A,a), because different choices of witnesses may lie in different automorphism orbits. However, every countable model of ψ(c), after forgetting c, is a copy of A, since ∃y ψ(y) is a Scott sentence. Thus, up to isomorphism of expansions by constants, there are only countably many such models, corresponding to countably many tuples in copies of A, and hence only countably many universal types realized among them. Use Lemma 5.3 inside the Π2 class of models of ψ(c) to omit all universal types in this class that are not existentially supported. The resulting model still satisfies ψ(c), so its reduct is isomorphic to A, and all its realized universal types over c are existentially supported. By the Section 4 proposition, this expansion is existentially atomic over c. Transporting the constants through an isomorphism gives that A is existentially atomic over a finite tuple of parameters.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure. The following are equivalent. (B1) A is existentially atomic over a finite set of parameters. (B2) A has an infinitary Σ3 Scott sentence. (B3) A is computably categorical on a cone: there is an oracle C such that, for every oracle X with X ≥_T C, every X-computable copy of A is X-computably isomorphic to A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence B1 iff B2 is Lemma 7.2. The implication B1 implies B2 is obtained by taking a Π2 Scott sentence for the finite-parameter expansion and existentially quantifying over the parameters. The converse uses the Π2 component of a satisfied Σ3 disjunct, expands by constants for a witness tuple, and applies type omission to get an existentially atomic expansion. The equivalence B1 iff B3 is cited as the boldface, or cone-relativized, version of the Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman and Chisholm theorem characterizing relative computable categoricity by effective existential atomicity over finitely many parameters.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable structure. The following are equivalent. (C1) A is effectively existentially atomic, i.e. has a c.e. Scott family of existential formulas. (C2) A is uniformly relatively computably categorical: there is a computable operator Φ such that for every copy B of A, Φ^{D(B)} is an isomorphism from B to A. (C3) A is uniformly computably categorical in the sense that the same kind of computable operator is required to work for computable copies B of A when given their atomic diagrams as oracles. (C4) A is uniformly effectively 1-prime: there is a computable operator that, for every computable model B of the ∀2-theory of A, outputs a 1-embedding A→B from D(B).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper attributes the equivalence of C1, C2, and C3 to Ventsov. The idea is that a c.e. existential Scott family allows a uniform effective back-and-forth construction of isomorphisms from any copy to A, while a uniform procedure for producing such isomorphisms can be converted back into c.e. existential orbit information by finite-use analysis. The equivalence C1 iff C4 is proved directly in Lemma 6.2: from a c.e. existential Scott family one uniformly builds 1-embeddings into models of the ∀2-theory, and from a uniform 1-prime operator one extracts finite atomic-diagram conditions that existentially support universal types and hence form a c.e. existential Scott family.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a Πc_2 class of structures such that no two non-isomorphic structures in K have the same existential theory. Then every structure A in K has enumeration degree given by its existential theory S = ∃-Th(A). Here A has enumeration degree S if every enumeration of S computes a copy of A and every copy of A computes an enumeration of S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Every copy of A can enumerate S by listing the existential sentences true in that copy. Conversely, suppose an oracle X computes an enumeration of S. By Corollary 5.2, X computes a presentation of some B in K whose existential theory is S. By the hypothesis on K, B is isomorphic to A. Therefore exactly the oracles enumerating S compute copies of A, so S is the enumeration degree of A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If A and B are existentially atomic countable structures with the same existential theory, then A and B are isomorphic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define I to be the set of pairs of finite tuples (a,b), with a from A and b from B of the same length, such that a and b have the same universal type in their respective structures. The empty tuples lie in I because the structures have the same existential theory and hence the same universal theory at the sentence level. If (a,b) is in I, then a and b have the same atomic diagram. For the forth step, take c in A. Let ψ(x,y) be an existential formula defining or supporting the orbit, equivalently the universal type, of the tuple a c in A. Since a and b have the same universal type, B cannot satisfy the universal formula ¬∃y ψ(b,y); otherwise A would satisfy ¬∃y ψ(a,y), contradicting c. Hence choose d in B with B models ψ(b,d). To prove (a c,b d) is in I, let θ(x,y) be any universal formula true of a c in A. In A, the formula ∀y(ψ(a,y) implies θ(a,y)) holds; after rewriting the implication from the existential ψ, this is a universal formula about a, so it also holds of b in B. Since B models ψ(b,d), B models θ(b,d). This gives one inclusion of universal types. For the reverse inclusion, take an existential formula ψ̃ generating the universal type of b d in B. The first inclusion implies that A models ψ̃(a,c), and the same argument with A and B interchanged yields the reverse inclusion. Thus I has the back-and-forth property, so A and B are isomorphic.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a Πc_2 class all of whose structures are existentially atomic. Then every structure A in K has an enumeration degree, and that enumeration degree is given by the existential theory ∃-Th(A).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 8.2, any two existentially atomic structures with the same existential theory are isomorphic. Therefore, within K, the existential theory determines the isomorphism type. Lemma 8.1 then applies: every copy of A enumerates ∃-Th(A), and any enumeration of ∃-Th(A) computes, via Corollary 5.2, a copy of a structure in K with the same existential theory, hence a copy of A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 8.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If a countable structure A has a computable infinitary Σc_3 Scott sentence, then A has an enumeration degree.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write the Σc_3 Scott sentence as a computable disjunction of sentences ∃x_i ψ_i(x_i), each ψ_i Πc_2. Since A satisfies one disjunct, that disjunct is itself a Scott sentence for A. Choose a tuple a in A witnessing ψ_i(a), expand the language by constants c for that tuple, and consider the Πc_2 condition ψ_i(c). The paper claims A has enumeration degree given by the existential type of a, equivalently the existential theory of the expansion (A,a). Every copy of A can enumerate this existential type. Conversely, from an enumeration of it, Corollary 5.2 builds a model of ψ_i(c) with that existential theory. Even if the resulting expansion is not isomorphic to (A,a), its reduct satisfies the Scott sentence ∃x_i ψ_i(x_i), so the reduct is isomorphic to A.

proof_techniques.name: Back-and-forth from existential orbit formulas
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly turns existential orbit definitions into isomorphisms by defining, for a candidate structure B, a relation I_B between tuples of the original structure A and tuples of B: (a,b) belongs to I_B iff B satisfies the existential formula φ_a that defines the orbit of a in A. The Scott sentence construction and several categoricity arguments then reduce to verifying that I_B has the back-and-forth property: matching atomic diagrams and extending on both sides by one element. Because existential formulas are upwardly witnessed by finite pieces of the atomic diagram, the resulting back-and-forth requirements have low infinitary complexity, typically Π2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A graph of an automorphism can often be described by saying that certain parameter-augmented tuples lie in the same orbit. If one can obtain existential orbit definitions over finite parameters a ∪ π(a), this technique is a route to Σ1-in definitions of automorphism graphs; conversely, failure of such orbit definitions is a natural obstruction to definability.

proof_techniques.name: Supporting universal types instead of defining full orbits directly
proof_techniques.description: Rather than constructing orbit formulas outright, Section 4 proves that it is enough to find, for every tuple a, an existential formula φ_a that supports the universal type of a, meaning that φ_a implies every universal formula true of a. The proof derives orbit definitions by showing that the relation A models φ_a(b) is symmetric and has the back-and-forth extension property. This converts a type-support condition into existential atomicity without first knowing that the formulas isolate complete first-order types.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a proposed structure satisfying an automorphism-computability hypothesis, it may be easier to show that finite parameter tuples have existentially supported universal types than to define full automorphism orbits. This would still be enough, by the paper's argument, to obtain existential orbit definitions that may feed into Σ1-in definitions of maps.

proof_techniques.name: Type omission for unsupported universal types in Π2 classes
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 5.3 gives a constructive type-omitting theorem tailored to universal types and Π2 classes. The construction builds a model by finite approximations, alternating between satisfying the Π2 axioms and diagonalizing against a listed type on a listed tuple. The key step is that if a universal type p is not existentially supported, then any current existential finite-diagram condition can be extended inside the class to force the failure of some universal formula in p; since the failure of a universal formula is existential, it is finitely witnessed and persists to the limit.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a useful contrapositive tool: if some tuple or parameter configuration lacks existential support, one can often build another copy or expansion omitting its universal type. Such a construction may be used to contradict a cone-categoricity or uniform automorphism-computability assumption.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-use/permutation extraction from uniform computable operators
proof_techniques.description: The right-to-left direction of Lemma 6.2 is one of the paper's most transferable arguments. Given a uniform computable operator producing embeddings, fix a computation on a tuple and record its finite oracle use s. The finite initial atomic diagram seen by the computation becomes an existential formula: another tuple satisfying that finite diagram can be moved into the first s positions by a computable permutation of the presentation, and the computation cannot distinguish the two presentations. Because the operator's outputs preserve universal types, the two tuples must share a universal type; hence the finite diagram formula existentially supports that type.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem asks about computability of automorphisms relative to presentations on a cone versus Σ1-in definability of automorphisms. This finite-use method is a direct template for converting a uniform procedure that computes maps from diagrams into existential formulas defining or supporting the relevant graph or orbit data, especially after permuting finite tuples in a copy.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-parameter expansion versus Σ3 Scott sentence
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses a clean parameter tradeoff: if an expansion (A,a) by finitely many constants is existentially atomic, then its Π2 Scott sentence becomes a Σ3 Scott sentence for A after existentially quantifying over the constants. Conversely, from a Σ3 Scott sentence, one chooses a satisfied Π2 disjunct with witness tuple, expands by constants for that tuple, and uses type omission to find an expansion in which all realized universal types are existentially supported.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original question quantifies over finite parameter sets and their images under automorphisms. This theorem suggests analyzing finite-parameter expansions of A and asking whether the relevant computability-on-a-cone property implies a Σ3 Scott description or existential atomicity for such expansions.

proof_techniques.name: Existential algebraicity via minimal finite solution formulas
proof_techniques.description: For existentially algebraic structures, Lemma 3.4 defines tuple orbits by choosing an existential formula true of the tuple with the minimum possible finite number of solutions. If another solution is not automorphic, Lemma 3.3 finds an existential formula distinguishing the two expanded structures, and one combines formulas to reduce the number of solutions, contradicting minimality. This is a finite-combinatorial way to get existential orbit definitions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Existentially algebraic structures provide a robust supply of examples where automorphism orbits are existentially definable. They may serve either as positive test cases for proposed implications or as components in constructions where one wants tightly controlled local orbits.

proof_techniques.name: Computable model construction from c.e. finite diagrams
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 5.1 constructs a computable model of a Πc_2 class K when the finite diagrams K_fin realized in K are c.e. The construction searches for finite extensions that meet more and more Π2 requirements, relying on the fact that any current finite diagram comes from an actual model of K. Corollary 5.2 refines this by adding Π2 axioms that force a prescribed existential theory S, yielding an X-computable model when S is c.e. in X.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a way to build computable or oracle-computable copies with controlled existential theory. In attempts to separate computability of automorphisms from Σ1-in definability, it may be useful for producing adversarial copies that preserve existential information while changing the behavior of automorphisms or parameter tuples.

proof_techniques.name: Existential theory as an isomorphism invariant for existentially atomic classes
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 8.2 shows that two existentially atomic structures with the same existential theory are isomorphic. The proof again uses a back-and-forth relation, but now tuples are matched by equality of universal types; existential atomicity supplies the formulas needed for the extension steps. Theorem 1.14 packages this into an enumeration-degree result for Πc_2 classes of existentially atomic structures.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a candidate construction is existentially atomic, then its existential theory may determine its entire isomorphism type. This can simplify cone arguments by reducing copies to enumerations of existential facts, but it also indicates that a counterexample to definability may need to exploit automorphism-level complexity not visible at the level of isomorphism type.

other_useful_info: Examples stated in the paper: a linear ordering is existentially atomic iff it is dense or finite; a field is existentially atomic iff it is algebraic over its prime subfield; algebraic fields, connected finite-valence graphs with a named root, and finite-dimensional torsion-free abelian groups with a named basis are existentially algebraic and hence existentially atomic. The introduction notes that every structure becomes existentially atomic after taking enough structural jumps: for Scott rank α, the (<α)-jump obtained by adding relations for computable infinitary Σβ formulas for β < α is existentially atomic relative to a suitable oracle. The paper cites Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman and Chisholm for the characterization of relative computable categoricity by effective existential atomicity over finitely many parameters, Ventsov for uniform computable categoricity, Cholak-McCoy for effective prime uniqueness, and Melnikov-Montalbán/Effros for the Polish-group-action perspective on Π2 Scott sentences and continuous categoricity.
why_relevant: Directly studies structures whose automorphism orbits of tuples are definable by existential formulas, the core model-theoretic notion behind Sigma-in-1 definability. Its equivalences with Pi-2 Scott sentences and effective categoricity are likely central to proving or refuting the proposed separation.
